steps.text: Recalibrate the gas dilution system once per calendar year using NIST-traceable primary flow standards with an uncertainty ≤ 0.25%.

Report the results of the calibration, listing the date of the most recent calibration, the due date for the next calibration, calibration point, reference flow device ID, S:N ratio, and acceptance criteria.
Verify the gas dilution system is non-biasing under HFC zero air and known standards at least once per calendar year for each reactive target compound.
Flow zero air through all applicable MFCs, tubing, and manifold used and verify it is not detectable for the target compounds.

Flow a known standard within the calibration range of the analytical system for each target compound through all applicable MFCs, tubing, and manifold to allow equalization and verify it does not bias the standard by ± 15% of the concentrations in the reference sample.
Verify the gas dilution system daily or per use (whichever is less stringent) per Section 3.2 of Method 205 using any available protocol gas and corresponding reference method.
Recalibrate the static gas dilution system once per calendar year using a NIST-traceable primary pressure gauge with an uncertainty ≤ 0.1%.

Verify the calibration of the hygrometer weekly or per use (whichever is less stringent) at a single point that is approximately 40 to 50% RH to within ± 5% using a second calibrated hygrometer or a saturated salt solution.
Evaluate working-level standards prepared in canisters within 30 days of preparation.
Retain and report the gas standard certificates of analysis.
Flush the sampling device or system with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air or ultrapure nitrogen for a minimum of 24 hours before field deployment.
Demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation (preconcentrator, GC-MS system, and all connections) is non-biasing under zero-air.
Use the analytical instrumentation to analyze humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air from a known clean source at the installation prior to initial use of the instrument.

Examine chromatograms for interferences and other chromatographic artifacts such as nontarget peak responses, large peaks or rises in the chromatogram due to undifferentiated compounds, and baseline anomalies.
Take steps to remove the contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Repeat the analytical zero-air verification to ensure that you have mitigated any problems before using the analytical instrumentation.
Demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation (preconcentrator, GC-MS system, and all connections) is non-biasing under the known-standard challenge.
Take steps to remove contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions (e.g., analyzing replicates of humidified clean gas).
Perform analytical known-standard challenge for analytical instrumentation to demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation is not causing loss of compounds.
Check for cold spots in transfer line, gas impurities, sorbent traps, GC column, system leaks, and/or poor moisture management.
Consult instrument manufacturer for steps to eliminate bias, as necessary.
Perform zero-air challenge of autosamplers associated with analytical instrument systems by connecting the clean humidified gas sample to each port and analyzing humidified zero air.
Heat and purge any lines, and/or rinse with deionized water, dry, and purge any lines that fail.
Choose the quantitation ion for each IS compound as the most abundant ion (base peak).
Invalidate then reanalyze any samples.
Analyze a sufficient number of humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air blanks or humidified check standards to verify that instrument sensitivity is stable.
Calibrate the GC-MS.
Prepare the calibration curve by preparing standards that bracket the expected concentration levels at the sampling location(s).

Include at least five levels in the ICAL to approximate concentrations of target oHAPs expected at the deployment location(s), including one level within a factor of five of the detection limits and another within 10% of the compound specific action-level.
Calculate the theoretical concentrations of the working calibration standards using the certified concentration from the gas vendor or neat standard provider.
Prepare a calibration curve for each target analyte by determining the relative response factor (RRF) of each concentration level.
Calculate the RRF of each target compound in each calibration level using Equation 11 in Section 12.
Choose the quantitation ion for each target compound as the most abundant ion (base peak) unless there is a spectral interference.
Review the sample collection information documented on the field data page and/or COC form(s) for completeness and accuracy.
Compare the canister label with the sample collection data sheet and verify that the canister and sample IDs are correct.
Measure and record the canister pressure using a calibrated vacuum/pressure gauge or transducer.
Flag the results as invalid.
Pressurize the canister with diluent gas to a pressure less than or equal to the final pressure of the standard gas canisters.
Measure the canister pressure using a calibrated vacuum/pressure gauge or pressure transducer just prior to dilution and immediately following dilution.
Calculate the canister dilution correction factor (CDCF) from the two absolute pressure readings.
Allow diluted canisters to equilibrate for a minimum of 12 hours before analysis.
Draw a measured aliquot of the whole air sample from the sample canister by vacuum through a preconcentrator.

preconditions.text: Following successful completion of the known-standard challenge
Prior to initial use and as part of an instrument’s annual calibration
If you identify exceedances of target compounds in the zero-air challenge
At installation prior to initial use of the instrument.
At installation prior to initial use of the instrument and with instrument’s annual calibration.
Prior to initial use, upon replacement of transfer lines, or after analysis of potentially contaminating samples.
The IS area response differs by more than 30% from the mean IS area response.
Calibration is required initially, annually, whenever CCV standards exceed acceptance criteria, or when the system is out of control as indicated by IS responses.
If pressure differences exceed ±3.5 kPa (1 in. Hg or 0.5 psi) of that measured upon collection in the field

constraints.text: Quadrupole and ion trap systems employing electron impact (EI) ionization mode must provide nominal 70 volt (V) electron energy in EI mode to produce a BFB mass spectrum meeting acceptance criteria.
The gas dilution system must produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
The equalization time for the bias verification must be used at a minimum for the development of standards.

Pressure transducer(s) or pressure gauge(s) used for static dilution must have an accuracy of ± 0.1% full scale or 0.13 kPa, whichever is smaller, calibrated over the range of use for the application with sufficient resolution to permit precise measurement of pressure differentials.

Connection tubing for the static dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel. Manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
The calibrated hygrometer must be capable of a 1% RH resolution with a yearly calibration to a NIST-traceable accuracy of ± 3% RH within the range of 20% to 80% RH.

You must use only NIST-certified or NIST-traceable calibration standards, standard reference materials, and reagents that are stable through certification and recertification for the tests and procedures required by this method.
You must use standards and reagents within their expiration period.
The analysis must show that the concentration of any detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample is < 20 pptv or undetected (whichever is more stringent) per compound of interest.
Analysis must show that any detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample are at response levels that are expected to be < 20 pptv or not detected.

Verifies that all target compounds are detected by the system, that they respond consistently upon repeated injection, and that they exhibit sufficient response to be quantifiable at low concentrations.
Each target VOC’s concentration must be < 20 pptv or preferably not detected.

If there is a spectral interference from a coeluting or nearby compound or interference that impacts the quantitation of the base peak, select another abundant ion that is distinguishable for quantitation.
Calibration standards prepared with neat materials must account for the standard purity when calculating the working standard concentrations.
The %RSD of the RRF for each target compound must be ≤ 30%.
The calculated concentration for each target compound(s) at each calibration level must be within ± 30% of the theoretical concentration when quantitated against the resulting calibration curve.
The measured canister absolute pressure must be within ±3.5 kPa (1 in. Hg or 0.5 psi) of that measured upon collection in the field.
